invariant_definition: A central Kashmir Śaiva term for the intrinsic pulsation of consciousness. *Spanda* does not mean gross physical vibration. It names the fact that ultimate consciousness is not inert self-presence but living self-articulation: a throb of manifestation, withdrawal, recognition, and agency within one awareness. The term therefore sits between ontology and practice. Metaphysically, it protects non-dualism from collapsing into static monism by insisting that the real is intrinsically dynamic. Practically, it gives a way to interpret experience, breath, attention, and moments of intensified awareness as expressions of the same divine pulsation. The concept is closely linked to *svātantrya*, *vimarśa*, *unmeṣa*, and *pratyabhijñā*: sovereign freedom manifests as pulsation, and liberation is the recognition of one's own awareness as that pulsation. Translating *spanda* merely as vibration is often too mechanical unless the reader is told that the doctrine concerns consciousness before it concerns motion.

per_school.definition: Classical Sāṃkhya does not here elevate spanda into an autonomous metaphysical principle; in the commentarial exposition on Sāṃkhya-Kārikā 29, spandanakarma denotes the common activity of the vital airs shared across the organ-complex (cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/29-commentary). In the available Sāṃkhya locus, then, spanda designates physiological or instrumental motion within prakṛti's evolutes, not the self-luminous dynamism of puruṣa (cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/29-commentary).

In the Yoga-Bhāṣya, the stem spand- marks the activation of the kleśas, which reinforce the dominion of the guṇas and set causal process in motion (cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.3). Elsewhere the bhāṣya uses parispandate for the embodied subject's mental, verbal, and bodily stirring in pursuit of pleasurable means (cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.15). Within the available Yoga material, therefore, spanda belongs to the register of psychophysical agitation inside saṃsāra rather than to a distinct ontological principle (cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.3; cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/2.15).

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika uses spanda in the technical orbit of motion (karma) and its perceptibility (cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.34; cite://udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/3.348). Vātsyāyana can treat 'the pot moves' (spandate) as an ordinary perceptual predication of a substance, which shows spanda functioning as a real, apprehended feature of things (cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/2.1.34). Udayana then systematizes the contrast by stating spando 'spanda iti dvayameva and glossing the pair respectively as karma and guṇa (cite://udayana/nyaya-kusumanjali/3.348).

per_school.definition: In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, spanda names the self-uccalana of consciousness, the pulsational unfolding and retraction through which the universe arises and subsides (cite://vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1; cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/4.183). The Spanda-Kārikā formulates this through Śaṅkara whose unmeṣa and nimeṣa are the world's dissolution and emergence, while Abhinavagupta explicitly defines spanda as the Self's own surging forth (cite://vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1; cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/4.183). Kṣemarāja integrates the same doctrine with Pratyabhijñā by grounding manifestation in autonomous citi, which unfolds the universe on its own surface, and by treating the finite subject as consciousness contracted into a limited world-form (cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1; cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/2; cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/4).

The present workspace contains no primary grammatical texts for this school, and spanda is not otherwise established here as a school-defining technical category.

translator_note: 'Pulsation' is often better than 'vibration,' and both need explanation. The lazy English gloss suggests a physical oscillation in space. The Sanskrit term instead names the self-articulating dynamism of consciousness itself. The most damaging mistranslation is to import a process-philosophy or proto-physics analogy as if Trika were describing a quantum-field substrate; *spanda* is not a wave-like motion of any substance but the intrinsic liveliness of *cit* prior to the subject-object division. Equally, do not confuse *spanda* with *vyāpāra* (operation, function): *vyāpāra* is the determinate activity of a faculty; *spanda* is the underlying self-articulation that makes any *vyāpāra* possible.
